coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional Additive Manufacturing and Multiphysics Numerical Investigations of Carbon Fiber Structural Battery Composite using a Drop-on-Demand method with In-situ Consolidation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Xiangyang
Chen, Yuekun
coredata.dc:description: Lightweight carbon fiber structural battery composite has great potential in increasing structural energy storage efficiency for multifunctional applications. However, it is still challenging to design carbon fiber multifunctional composite due to lack of proper manufacturing methods. In this study, an integrated multifunctional design and fabrication approach is developed by combining a drop-on-demand additive manufacturing method with a multiphysics numerical model to guide the development of the new multifunctional composite. Through deposition with in-situ consolidation, the function and thickness of each carbon fiber layer as well as its fiber volume fraction are accurately controlled. Decreasing layer thickness improves flexural properties. The discharge capacity and energy density are initially improved by 47% and 39%, respectively, but then significantly reduced due to limited lithium transport within closely packed carbon fibers at higher volume fraction. An optimal fiber volume fraction of 35 vol% exists for carbon fiber structural battery composite. A good agreement is found between prediction results and experimental measurements. Further investigations of lithium-ion concentration and stress distribution by the validated multiphysics model find that electrolyte conductivity more significantly affects discharge process than discharge rate. The multifunctional design and fabrication approach provides insights for future development of high-performance carbon fiber structural battery composite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Structural battery composite
Additive manufacturing
Multifunctional design
In-situ consolidation
Multifunctional performance
Multiphysics modeling

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid fundingbodyid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder sponsoredaccesstype srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Insulation photopolymer and solid polymer electrolyte (SPE)

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Air Force Office of Scientific Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from AFOSR grant FA9550-21-1-0226.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional architecture of carbon fiber structural battery composites: (a) shows the schematics of laminated composite architecture with carbon fiber fabrics. (b) highlights the internal structural battery components. (c) shows a typical experimentally 3D printed structural battery composite sample prepared with commercial carbon fiber weave fabrics for comparison.

Schematics of 3D printing processes for carbon fiber structural battery composites: (a) shows the preparation of EPD coating of carbon fiber fabrics with binder-free cathode materials. (b) shows a drop-on-demand method to additively manufacture carbon fiber structural battery composites layer-by-layer. (c) shows post-processing of the 3D printed structural battery composites. (d) demonstrates typical 3D printed structural battery composite samples with various complex geometries.
Schematics of multiphysics modeling 3D printed carbon fiber structural battery composites.

Microstructure of each functional layer for additively manufactured structural powered composite: (a) presents a close-up view of the positive electrode layer. Active material (LFP) is observed as bright particles distributed within conductive materials (carbon black and graphene) as dark phase. (b) shows a close-up view of the SPE phase, indicating the presence of nanoporous structure that works as lithium-ion pathway. (c) shows a close-up view of the pure photopolymer phase, working as insulation layer for the 3D printed structural battery composites. (d) shows the relationship between print layer thickness and obtained fiber volume fraction of 3D printed carbon fiber structural battery composites.

Characterization of mechanical and electrochemical properties of 3D printed structural battery composites in terms of print layer thickness: (a) shows the typical stress–strain curves from flexural tests. (b) compares the measured flexural modulus and strength. (c) shows the typical time-dependent voltage curves during discharge processes at varying print layer thickness. (d) compares the measured specific capacities and energy densities normalized to the cathode active materials. The in-situ consolidation improves the obtained multifunctional performance but may lower the electrochemical properties at lower print layer thickness.

Predicted lithium-ion concentrations during discharge processes for the 3D printed structural battery composites: (a) shows the predicted lithium-ion concentration at a SOC of 0.50 for a structural battery composite sample with a print layer thickness of 0.4 mm. (b) shows the predicted discharge curves for structural battery composites with print layer thickness of 0.7 mm and 0.4 mm. The experimentally measured discharge curves are also included for comparison and validation of the developed multiphysics model. (c) shows the lithium-ion concentration distribution across fiber layers at different SOCs. (d) shows the predicted lithium-ion concentration at a SOC of 1.00 for a structural battery composite sample with a print layer thickness of 0.4 mm. (e) and (f) show the typical lithium-ion concentration within a carbon fiber at different SOCs along x- and y-directions, respectively.

Comparison of lithium-ion concentration within carbon fiber negative electrode and electrochemical properties of 3D printed structural battery composites with different print layer thickness. (a)-(c) show the predicted lithium-ion concentration at a SOC of 1.00 for print layer thickness of 0.7 mm, 0.4 mm, and 0.3 mm, respectively. (d) compares the lithium-ion concentration distribution across fiber layers for structural battery composites 3D printed with different layer thickness. Decreasing layer thickness initially facilitates delithiation of more fiber layers. However, further decreased layer thickness lowers the number of fiber layers delithiated during discharge. (e) and (f) summarize the predicted discharge capacity and energy densities with different layer thickness. The experimentally measured data are also included for comparison and validation of simulation results.

Multiphysics modeling of stress distribution within carbon fiber negative electrode during discharge processes for 3D printed structural battery composites with varying print layer thickness. (a) compares the stress (σ11) distribution within 3D printed structural battery composites with layer thickness of 0.7 mm, 0.4 mm, and 0.3 mm at a SOC of 1.00. The stress (σ11) is concentrated within fiber layers starting delithiation. (b) highlights typical stress distribution (σ11) within a single carbon fiber. (c) and (d) compares the maximum and minimum stresses of σ11 and σ22 within carbon fiber negative electrode during discharge with respect to print layer thickness. (e) compares the stress (σ22) distribution within 3D printed structural battery composites with layer thickness of 0.7 mm, 0.4 mm, and 0.3 mm at a SOC of 1.00. Similar to σ11, the stress (σ22) is concentrated within fiber layers starting delithiation. (f) highlights typical stress distribution (σ22) within a single carbon fiber.

Multiphysics modeling and comparison of multifunctional performance of 3D printed structural battery composites at layer thickness of 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm with ionic conductivities of 2.0 mS/cm and 1.0 S/cm. (a) compares lithium-ion concentration within carbon fiber negative electrode at layer thickness of 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm during discharge with an ionic conductivity of 1.0 S/cm. (b) summarizes lithium-ion concentrations across fiber layers. Increasing ionic conductivity yields more uniform distribution of lithium-ion concentrations. (c) compares the discharge curves of 3D printed structural battery composites withing varying ionic conductivities. Varying layer thickness shows no obvious effects at an ionic conductivity of 1.0 S/cm. (d) compares stress (σ11) distribution within carbon fiber negative electrode at layer thickness of 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm during discharge with an ionic conductivity of 1.0 S/cm. Increasing ionic conductivity obviously contributes to more uniform stress distribution. (e) and (f) compare the predicted maximum stress at a SOC of 1.00 for σ11 and σ22, respectively. Increasing ionic conductivity obviously lowers the maximum stress for structural battery composites 3D printed with different layer thickness.

Multiphysics modeling and comparison of multifunctional performance of 3D printed structural battery composites at varying discharge rates. (a) shows lithium-ion concentrations at 0.2C for 3D printed samples with layer thickness of 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm. (b) compares lithium-ion concentrations across fiber layers for 3D printed samples with layer thickness of 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm at 1C and 0.2C. Lowering discharge rates obviously results in more uniform distribution of lithium-ion concentration during discharge. (c) compares the discharge curves of 3D printed composites with layer thickness of 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm at 1C and 0.2C. (d) shows the stress distribution (σ11) at 0.2C for 3D printed samples with layer thickness of 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm. (e) and (f) compares the predicted maximum stress at a SOC of 1.00 for σ11 and σ22, respectively. Lowering discharge rate obviously lowers the maximum stress for structural battery composites with different layer thickness. Smaller layer thickness still yields relatively higher maximum stress values.
Multi-physics modeling framework to study coupled electrochemical-mechanical performance.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional Additive Manufacturing and Multiphysics Numerical Investigations of Carbon Fiber Structural Battery Composite using a Drop-on-Demand method with In-situ Consolidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Missouri University of Science and Technology, Rolla, MO 65409, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Missouri University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Missouri University of Science and Technology, Rolla, MO 65409, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lightweight carbon fiber structural battery composite has great potential in increasing structural energy storage efficiency for multifunctional applications. However, it is still challenging to design carbon fiber multifunctional composite due to lack of proper manufacturing methods. In this study, an integrated multifunctional design and fabrication approach is developed by combining a drop-on-demand additive manufacturing method with a multiphysics numerical model to guide the development of the new multifunctional composite. Through deposition with in-situ consolidation, the function and thickness of each carbon fiber layer as well as its fiber volume fraction are accurately controlled. Decreasing layer thickness improves flexural properties. The discharge capacity and energy density are initially improved by 47% and 39%, respectively, but then significantly reduced due to limited lithium transport within closely packed carbon fibers at higher volume fraction. An optimal fiber volume fraction of 35 vol% exists for carbon fiber structural battery composite. A good agreement is found between prediction results and experimental measurements. Further investigations of lithium-ion concentration and stress distribution by the validated multiphysics model find that electrolyte conductivity more significantly affects discharge process than discharge rate. The multifunctional design and fabrication approach provides insights for future development of high-performance carbon fiber structural battery composite.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapidly increasing demand in mobile electric technologies makes it necessary to develop lightweight structures and materials to enable longer service life and operating distance. Compared to current strategies in minimizing the weight of energy storage materials, an alternative approach is to integrate functions of energy storage and load bearings into a single composite material (i.e., structural battery composite). Commonly used as lightweight structural materials, carbon fiber reinforced composites (CFRCs) show great potentials of simultaneously carrying mechanical loads while storing electrical energy [1]. The integrated structural energy storage can provide auxiliary electrical energy to sensors and other onboard microelectronics without the need of additional power sources. Thus, if successfully developed, they will offer significant reduction of weight and volume in a system level and achieve “massless” energy storage for electrically powered structural systems, significantly improving operation distance of electric vehicles and aircrafts. The developed multifunctional composite can even be extended to strain sensing [2], energy harvesting [2–5], and morphing [6].

However, the design and fabrication of these multifunctional materials present new challenges, which extend beyond the scope of conventional manufacturing approaches, e.g., composite lay-up fabrication process. Two types of carbon fiber structural battery composite design have been proposed so far, i.e., a laminated structural battery composite and a 3D carbon fiber structural battery design. As a common architecture used for composite laminates [7] and conventional batteries, most of the state-of-the-art carbon fiber multifunctional composites [8,9] are based on a laminated battery structure suitable for conventional CFRC lay-up fabrication process [7,10]. The laminated battery design was either achieved through embedding the thin-film lithium-ion batteries into CFRCs [11,12] or using carbon fiber sheets as battery components (e.g. current collectors [13,14] and negative electrodes [10,15]). However, accurate design and control of CFRC structure and multifunctional properties pose great challenges for existing fabrication methods. While current strategies toward this end have focused on improving battery chemistry and cell-level energy densities, combined experimental and numerical approaches are needed to achieve a fundamental understanding of the novel structural battery composite and fully explore CFRC multifunctionalities.

Various modeling frameworks have been developed to guide the design of the new multifunctional carbon fiber composites. An electro-chemo-mechanical coupled multiphysics model [16] was developed by considering the lithium diffusion and migration as well as stress-assisted ionic transport. Both galvanostatic and potentiostatic discharge conditions were calculated for the half-cell of carbon fiber structural battery composites. The effect of state of charge (SOC) on the composite elastic properties was studied by a micromechanical analytical model [17], showing that fiber volume fraction had a significant impact on the effective elastic modulus. The heat generated during structural battery cycling was also found to affect internal residual stress by a semi-analytical modeling framework [18] as well as a coupled thermo–electro–chemo–mechanical model [19]. However, very few of these numerical studies were validated against experimental investigations due to the challenges of fabricating corresponding composite structure, e.g., varied fiber volume fraction, different electrode layer thickness, and complete structural battery composite samples for experimental tests.

Recently, a combined numerical and experimental study [20] was performed in the characterization of multifunctional carbon fiber electrodes. A 2D multiphysics model was used to predict the time-dependent voltage curves and compared with the experimental measurements at varying charge/discharge rates. It was found that increasing the volume fraction of carbon fiber or coating materials increased capacity and discharge time. However, due to the limitation of experimental condition, only a liquid electrolyte was used in characterizing electrode performance, limiting its further application in a complete structural battery composite that needs to use solid electrolyte. To address the limitation, another recent work [21] compared the multifunctional performance of carbon fiber electrode embedded within solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) and liquid electrolyte through combined experimental and multiphysics numerical techniques. The multifunctional performance of the structural battery samples was highly affected by the electrolyte properties. Due to the lower conductivity of SPE, the capacities of the SPE samples were reduced compared to those using liquid electrolyte. The reduction in the obtained capacities was higher after increasing electrode layer thickness. Noticeable residual strain was also found attributed to the partly constrained fiber deformation within the SPE. However, in accordance with the experimental condition, only a fixed fiber volume fraction of 20% was investigated.

As discussed above, while various numerical studies have been performed in predicting the performance of structural battery composites, a grand challenge lies in the limitation of proper manufacturing methods that can flexibly design and control multifunctional composite structure. To this end, CFRCs have recently gained much attention for their potentials in additive manufacturing (AM) techniques, which enable enormous flexibility in structure design and fabrication of new composite structure. Coextrusion deposition technique is commonly used to 3D print CFRCs via in-nozzle impregnation [22–26]. However, the existing AM techniques are mainly optimized for CFRC mechanical performance [17–19]. Only a few studies have implemented AM techniques to achieve CFRC multifunctionality in load bearings and energy storage. Previous studies [27–29] introduced coextrusion deposition-based AM methods to fabricate 3D structural battery composites with continuous carbon fiber. These AM methods provide an alternative approach to control structural battery composite structure. However, no further numerical investigation was performed to analyze the corresponding multifunctional performance. It is thus of great interest to develop integrated numerical and experimental AM approaches to guide the design and fabrication of high-performance multifunctional carbon fiber composites.

In this study, an integrated multifunctional design and fabrication approach is developed by combining a drop-on-demand AM method with a multiphysics numerical model to guide the development of the new structural battery composite. The new drop-on-demand AM approach is developed to accurately control the structure of a laminated carbon fiber structural battery composite. Through selectively depositing functional layers, a complete multifunctional structural battery composite is 3D printed with both battery and insulation layers in Fig. 1 , essential for fully functional components. For the first time, a facile method to accurately control each functional layer thickness and fiber volume fraction is achieved by an integrated in-situ consolidation technique with commercial carbon fiber weave fabrics. The mechanical and electrochemical properties are experimentally characterized by varying functional layer thickness and fiber volume fraction from 18 vol% to 54 vol%. A multiphysics numerical model is also developed to investigate the multifunctional performance of 3D printed structural battery composite. The prediction results and the experimental measurements are first compared for validation. The numerical model is then used to elucidate the effect of varying functional layer thickness on obtained multifunctional performance, including discharge curves, lithium-ion concentration, and stress distribution. Further numerical studies are also performed to evaluate the effects of electrolyte conductivity and discharge rate on the composite performance to guide future development of high-performance carbon fiber structural battery composite.

Commercial Polyacrylonitrile (PAN)-based plain weave carbon fiber fabric (6 K T830, Toray) was chosen due to their favorable electrochemical and mechanical properties. The carbon fiber fabric was activated through desizing by soaking it in Acetone for 8 h and in a chloroform bath overnight. The desized carbon fiber fabric was then dried at 80 °C for at least 8 h to remove residual chloroform. A copper foil current collector and an aluminum foil current collector were adhered to the desized carbon fiber fabrics using silver conductive epoxy adhesive for negative electrode and positive electrode, respectively, as shown in Fig. S1.

A binder-free positive electrode was coated on desized carbon fiber via electrophoretic deposition (EPD) in Fig. 2 (a), consisting of lithium iron phosphate (LFP < 5 µm particle size, Sigma Aldrich), carbon black (Super P* conductive, Alfa Aesar), and graphene (Reduced graphene oxide, Cheap Tubes). LFP was used as electrochemically active material while carbon black and graphene were used as carbon-based conductive materials. Following the previous work [30], the EPD method was used in this study to facilitate coating of binder-free cathode materials directly on carbon fibers and promote electrochemical performance. The EPD solution was prepared by first dispersing 500 mg of LFP in dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent using an ultrasonic homogenizer operating at 30 kHz. The surface charge of LFP was modified by slowly adding 0.5 mL poly diallyldimethylammonium chloride (PDDA 20 wt% in water, Sigma Aldrich) dissolved in ethanol over a period of 20 min during the ultrasonic homogenization treatment. This provided a positive surface charge, which would allow LFP to be attracted to the negatively charged carbon fiber fabric during EPD. Next, a suspension containing the conductive materials was prepared. Here, 25 mg graphene and 27 mg carbon black were added to 50 mL DMF. Finally, the EPD suspension was completed by mixing the LFP and conductive suspensions with ultrasonic homogenization for 20 min. The final EPD suspension was electrocoated onto the desized carbon fiber fabric via the EPD method. The setup consisted of a carbon fiber working electrode and stainless-steel counter electrode (15 mm × 90 mm) suspended into the EPD solution. During EPD coating, there was a separation distance of 40 mm between working and counter electrodes. EPD coating was performed using a Keithley 2400 sourcemeter. Our preliminary testing revealed that an applied DC potential of 70 V for 20 min resulted in deposition of a thin and uniform cathode (i.e., LFP) coating (about 0.001 g/cm2) on each carbon fiber and was thus used in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insulation photopolymer and solid polymer electrolyte (SPE)
ce:section.ce:para: Photopolymer (LOCTITE 3D 3955) was selected as insulation material due to its high performance as flame retardant and high modulus for structural applications. A bi-continuous solid polymer electrolyte was prepared by co-curing photopolymer and a 1:1 (by volume) solution of ethylene carbonate (EC) and propylene carbonate (PC) with 1 M LiClO4 in Fig. 2(b) during 3D printing. The electrolyte composition was selected due to its stable ionic conductivity over a long period of time [29]. LiClO4 was selected due to its less sensitivity to atmospheric moisture. Preliminary studies showed that an optimal 25 vol%:75 vol% of photopolymer and electrolyte was obtained with an iconic conductivity of 2.0 mS/cm and mechanical integrity suitable for 3D printing, and thus was selected in this study. It is worth noting that further decreasing the ratio of photopolymer would promote electrochemical performance but resulted in poor mechanical integrity, and vice versa.

A 3D printing process shown in Fig. 2(b), inspired by laminated object manufacturing, was implemented in this study. The fabrication process was achieved by a drop-on-demand method through adding different solutions at different functional layers, i.e., pure photopolymer for insulation layer and SPE for separator and electrode layers. The bonding was achieved by a laser curing process assisted with in-situ consolidation. During the 3D printing process, as shown in Fig. 2(b), the 3D CAD geometry was first sliced into 2D profiles for each layer and then cut by an ultra-violet laser (Snapmaker 10 W laser at a wavelength of 450 nm) with a laser spot size of 0.2 mm in diameter. To avoid damaging carbon fibers and short-circuiting, each carbon fiber fabric was added together with glass fiber separator (1080 style plain weave E-glass fabric by Fiberglass Supply with a surface weight of ∼ 47 g/m2 and a thickness of ∼ 56 µm) as a whole print layer in Fig. 2(b). Depending on the functions of each print layer, different solutions were selectively added following the scanning path of the sliced CAD geometry. To ensure full impregnation of fiber fabrics, at least five minutes were waited as experimentally determined following our previous studies [4,5]. Each deposition layer was then consolidated by a stainless roller (20 mm diameter) before being cured by an ultra-violet laser (Snapmaker 500 mW laser at a wavelength of 405 nm) at a 60° inclination angle from the build plate. The mechanical rolling (i.e., in-situ consolidation) was used here to control each functional layer thickness and fiber volume fraction. A laser-roller distance of 4 mm was used in this study. A laser power of 500 mW at 405 nm wavelength was used due to the curing behavior of the selected photopolymer materials (LOCTITE 3D 3955). The build plate moved at 150 mm/s in a serpentine pattern to print the entire layer with a 0.5 mm spacing between scanning lines. It should be noted that in this study, the negative electrode layer (i.e., including carbon fiber fabric and glass fiber fabric separator) was first added, consolidated, and cured before the positive electrode layer (i.e., including cathode coated carbon fiber fabric and glass fiber fabric separator) was deposited in a layer-by-layer fashion in Fig. 2(b). The 3D printing process was performed inside a glove box in an Argon atmosphere (<1 ppm oxygen content and moisture at ambient temperature). All prepared samples were then post-cured in a vacuum oven at 90 ℃ for 1 hr as shown in Fig. 2 (c). Fig. 2(d) shows several 3D printed samples for carbon fiber structural battery composite with different complex geometries via laser cutting of carbon fiber fabrics, demonstrating the potentials of the proposed 3D printing method in obtaining flexible forms of multifunctional components.

The morphology of structural battery composite samples was characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 600F Environmental Scanning Electron Microscope). All samples were sputtered coated with 25 nm platinum to improve image resolution. Mechanical properties of the printed structural battery composite samples were measured using an Instron 5881 tester, following ASTM D7264/D7264M-21 standard. Flexural strength and modulus were measured through three-point bending tests. Four samples were tested and averaged for each condition to determine the flexural properties. The flexural modulus is calculated by
E f
=
L
3
m
4 b
d
3

where
L
is the support span. A support span of 40 mm was used at a crosshead speed of 1 mm/min.
m
is the slope of the initial straight-line portion of the load deflection curve.
b
and
d
are the width and thickness of tested samples, respectively. 3D printed samples were prepared with a length of 60 mm and a width of 13 mm. The thickness of 3D printed samples was measured depending on the layer thickness used for each condition. The galvanostatic charge–discharge curves of composite samples were obtained using a Gamry Reference 600 + potentiostat. 10 mm by 10 mm samples were prepared and tested. The capacities of the 3D printed structural batteries were normalized to the cathode active materials. The tests were conducted within cut-off range between 3.5 V and 0.5 V, following previous work [14] using similar composite structure and compositions.

To investigate the effect of print layer thickness on the coupled multifunctional performance of the 3D printed structural battery composites, a 2D coupled electrochemical-mechanical model developed in COMSOL Multiphysics was used to improve the computation efficiency and analyze time-dependent lithium-ion concentrations, SOC, and corresponding stress distribution during discharge. Carbon fibers worked as current collector within positive electrode and were used as both current collector and electrode materials within negative electrode after being embedded within SPE and separated by separator. To model these structural battery details, a heterogeneous model was developed as shown in Fig. 3 to explicitly include carbon fiber structural battery composite architecture. Carbon fibers were evenly distributed and embedded within electrodes. The distance between these closely packed fibers was controlled by the fiber volume fraction, which was further controlled by the print layer thickness during the 3D printing experiments in this study. A radius of 3.5 µm was used for each carbon fiber. Preliminary convergency studies showed that a simulation domain of 19 × 3 fibers yielded converged prediction results and were thus adopted in this study.

The battery chemistry within SPE was modeled by the concentrated battery electrolyte charge and ion transport accounting for lithium-ion diffusion in Eq. (1) in Table 1 . The molecular flux of lithium within the electrodes was modeled by Fick’s law. The charge transfer reaction between the electrode and electrolyte phase was modeled by interfacial transfer kinetics in Eq. (2). The effect of lithium insertion in the carbon fibers was modeled using Eq. (3) to account for insertion strain and corresponding stress distribution. The stress-induced ionic transport was neglected in this study. The ohmic drop due to the current conduction within each carbon fiber was also explicitly included. The state of discharge was characterized by lithium-ion concentration, which accounted for the effect of its nonuniform distribution within the heterogeneous microstructure of the carbon fiber composite in Fig. 3. The properties of fibers and SPE were considered to be homogeneous, respectively, with their parameters obtained from previous similar work [16] for numerical modeling in this study.

The developed multiphysics model was then implemented in finite element analysis to investigate the coupled electrochemical-mechanical responses, including time-dependent lithium-ion concentration distribution, cell potential, discharge capacities, energy densities, and diffusion induced stress distribution, with respect to SOC, variation of print layer thickness, electrolyte ionic conductivity, and discharge rate as detailed below. A constant discharge current condition was implemented, consistent with experimental testing setup. The prediction results were compared with experimental measurements to validate the developed multiphysics model.

The microstructure of each phase within the 3D printed structural battery composites was first examined as shown in Fig. 4 . The coated cathode materials are shown in Fig. 4(a), showing a mixture of binder-free LFP (active), carbon black and graphene (conductive) materials. A porous SPE microstructure is also observed in Fig. 4(b), confirming that the combined (intermediate) laser curing and post heat treatment were able to achieve phase-separation. The obtained nanopores would work as pathways for lithium-ion transport during electrochemical reactions, while the solid phase worked as structural support materials. Using the proposed drop-on-demand AM method, pure photopolymer resin was added within the top and bottom layers in Fig. 1(b) to insulate the structural battery composites, as further confirmed by a void-free solid phase observed in Fig. 4(c). Through the integrated in-situ consolidation technique, the 3D print layer thickness was accurately controlled, yielding differently packed carbon fibers and thus varying fiber volume fraction. As calculated in Fig. 4(d), decreasing print layer thickness from 0.7 mm to 0.3 mm would increase the obtained fiber volume fraction from 18 vol% to 54 vol%. Four different print layer thickness, i.e., 0.7 mm, 0.5 mm, 0.4 mm, and 0.3 mm, were selected in the 3D printing experiments, and the corresponding multifunctional properties were also measured.

The mechanical performance was first compared in Fig. 5 . Decreasing print layer thickness through in-situ consolidation obviously improved the obtained flexural properties as seen from the measured flexural stress–strain curves in Fig. 5(a). As expected, decreasing print layer thickness increased fiber volume fraction and thus increased the obtained flexural properties. The measured flexural modulus and strength were improved by 16-fold and 6-fold, respectively, after decreasing the layer thickness from 0.7 mm to 0.3 mm. In the meantime, in-situ consolidation not only increased mechanical properties but also affected electrochemical performance. The voltage curves obtained during discharge were first compared for 3D printed structural battery composite samples with varying print layer thickness. In our preliminary tests, the 3D printed samples with a layer thickness of 0.4 mm were found in Fig. S2 to show stable electrochemical discharge processes with no obvious capacity fading up to 50 cycles, demonstrating the potentials of the proposed insulation layers. After stabilization, an average specific capacity of 89.5 mAh/g at 1C was obtained with good capacity retention observed. The capacity was normalized to the mass of cathode active materials. The average energy density was 152 Wh/kg with a nominal voltage of 1.55 V during discharge. With a low mass loading of cathode materials (i.e., LFP) at 0.001 g/cm2, the battery cell yielded an average discharge capacity of 4.08 mAh/g and an average energy density of 6.93 Wh/kg. It is worth noting that increasing the cathode mass loading could further increase the full battery cell performance but may yield nonuniform coating on carbon fiber fabric. Future studies will thus be needed to achieve a more uniformly cathode coating on carbon fiber fabric while still increasing cathode loading, e.g., spreading carbon fiber fabric before coating or reducing the bundle size of carbon fiber fabric.

Since the electrochemical capacity was found to stabilize within 10 cycles, all the samples were tested for 10 cycles at 1C and the 10th cycle results were used for comparative studies in Fig. 5(c). After decreasing print layer thickness, the consolidated composite structure is expected to facilitate a facile transport of lithium ions via a shorter diffusion path and thus enhance electrochemical reaction in Fig. 5(c). The corresponding discharge capacities and energy densities, normalized to the cathode active materials, were also found to be improved by 47% and 39%, respectively, after decreasing the print layer thickness from 0.7 mm to 0.4 mm. However, further decreasing the print layer thickness to 0.3 mm reversed the trend and lower the obtained discharge capacities and energy densities. Previous studies [11] suggested that a higher consolidation may damage the battery components. In contrast, all consolidated samples at a layer thickness of 0.3 mm in this study still showed a stable charge–discharge performance after 10 cycles. Thus, the multiphysics model was implemented below to further examine the effect of layer thickness on electrochemical performance of the 3D printed structural battery composites.

It is also worth noting that the obtained mechanical and electrochemical properties of the composite samples were still relatively low despite the usage of intermediate modulus carbon fibers (T830, Toray) [31]. It was mainly attributed to the relatively low mechanical properties of SPE used in this study. Only 25% photopolymer was used in the SPE. With an elastic modulus of 3.6 GPa for the photopolymer (LOCTITE 3D 3955), the effective elastic modulus of SPE was only about 0.9 GPa. The effective modulus of the composite samples may further be lowered by poor interfacial bonding (e.g., fiber/cathode, fiber/SPE, and cathode/SPE) due to the unique SPE percolating structure [32] (i.e., a nano-scale porous network of the solid polymer phase as also observed in Fig. 4(b)). It is thus worth future investigations on how to improve composite multifunctional properties, e.g., enhancement of SPE and interfacial bonding.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the lithium-ion concentration distribution within the structural battery composites at a SOC of 0.50 obtained from the multiphysics simulation. During the discharge, the lithium-ion concentration tended to be higher toward the middle region (i.e., separator) for the positive electrode, showing more discharge (lithiation for the positive electrode). As each carbon fiber worked as current collector, the location closer to the middle region thus was more accessible for charge transfer. A similar trend was also seen for the negative electrode. The time-dependent cell voltage predicted by the multiphysics model during the discharge simulation is also shown in Fig. 6(b). The prediction results were compared with the experimental measurements at layer thickness of 0.7 mm and 0.4 mm as shown in Fig. 6(b), where a good agreement was found, thus validating the developed multiphysics model. It is worth noting that each carbon fiber worked as both negative electrode and current collector. The first few carbon fiber layers in Fig. 6(a) toward the middle region tended to first start delithiation due to more accessible location for charge transfer. A lower lithium-ion concentration (higher SOC) was thus reached. To characterize the variation within carbon fiber layers, the average concentration at the center of each fiber layers within the negative electrode is also obtained and plotted in Fig. 6(c), showing a significant concentration gradient starting from the first carbon fiber layer. As the SOC increased, the concentration gradient shifted toward the carbon fiber layers further away from the middle region as more carbon fibers were delithiated during discharge. A corresponding lithium-ion concentration distribution at the SOC of 1.00 is shown in Fig. 6(d). Before all the carbon fiber layers were delithiated, the cell potential reached the lower cutoff voltage of 0.5 V and thus stopped the discharge process. This also helped explain the relatively lower discharge specific capacity in comparison with the theoretical value (170 mAh/g). While lithium-ion tended to be concentrated toward the middle region for the positive electrode, the discharge process of the whole structural battery composites was dominated by the number of delithiated carbon fiber layers within the negative electrode before reaching the lower cutoff voltage. Thus, further studies mainly focused on comparing the carbon fiber layers used as negative electrode during discharge.

A close-up view of the lithium-ion concentration within a typical carbon fiber during discharge was also analyzed along x-direction in Fig. 6(e) and y-direction in Fig. 6(f). Due to the circular shape of carbon fiber as negative electrode, delithiation started from the outer edge and gradually reached inner region as the SOC increased. While the delithiation process was symmetrical along the y-direction, the lithium-ion concentration along the x-direction tended to be lower toward the separator due to its more accessible location for charge transfer during discharge.

The effect of print layer thickness was further studied, which resulted in differently closely packed carbon fiber layers in Fig. 7 and different fiber volume fractions. The lithium-ion concentration distribution at a SOC of 1.00 was first compared for print layer thickness of 0.7 mm, 0.4 mm, and 0.3 mm in Fig. 7(a)-7(c). Varying print layer thickness mainly affected the total of number fibers that were delithiated as shown in Fig. 7(d). Decreasing layer thickness gradually increased the number of carbon fiber layers that were delithiated, reaching 18th layer for print thickness of 0.4 mm due to decreased ion transport distance. However, further decreasing layer thickness reversed the trend with only the 9th layer reached for the layer thickness of 0.3 mm. Although greatly reduced layer thickness facilitated lithium-ion transport with shorter distance, closely packed carbon fiber layers are believed to start limiting lithium-ion transport with smaller gap left between neighboring carbon fibers due to relatively low SPE ionic conductivity. This was further confirmed by comparing the effect of ionic conductivity on the electrochemical performance below.

The corresponding discharge capacities and energies were also calculated with respect to varying print thickness as summarized in Fig. 7(e) and 7(f), respectively. Decreasing layer thickness first improved the obtained capacities and energy densities. However, the trend was reversed after further decreasing print thickness. An optimal layer thickness of 0.4 mm with a fiber volume fraction of 35 vol% was obtained from the developed multiphysics predictive model. The prediction results were also validated in comparison with the experimental measurements of 3D printed structural battery composite samples at four different print layer thicknesses as shown in Fig. 7(e) and 7(f). It is worth noting that decreasing layer thickness here increased the fiber volume fraction as calculated in Fig. 4(d). Previous studies [21] used a fixed fiber volume fraction following the experimental setup. In comparison, this is the first study explicitly investigating and validating the effect of fiber volume fraction on the carbon fiber structural battery composites with the integrated multiphysics numerical model and the 3D printing method. As seen in Fig. 5(b), a higher fiber volume fraction was typically preferred due to improved mechanical performance at smaller print layer thickness. In contrast, while increasing fiber volume fraction may facilitate lithium-ion transport and result in higher electrochemical capacities, greatly reduced gap between closely packed carbon fibers may also negatively affect the electrochemical performance. This is believed to be uniquely related to the relatively low SPE ionic conductivity. The SPE ionic conductivity was thus varied below to explicitly examine its effect on structural battery composites printed with varying layer thickness.

The resulting stress distribution due to lithium-ion concentration was studied as shown in Fig. 8 . The predicted stress values, i.e., σ11 in Fig. 8(a) and σ22 in Fig. 8(e), were found to be concentrated in the regions showing higher gradient of lithium-ion concentrations, indicating that the stress was dominated by delithiation during discharge. A close-up examination of the stress distribution within a typical carbon fiber is shown in Fig. 8(b) for σ11 and in Fig. 8(f) for σ22. As the lithium-ion concentration was lower at the outer region during discharge, tensile stress was present near the outer surface with compressive stress near the middle area. Decreasing layer thickness would not obviously alter the stress concentration distribution. However, with closely packed carbon fibers at the layer thickness of 0.3 mm, higher tensile stress was found within the SPE matrix materials near the region further away from the separator due to higher lithium-ion concentrations. The maximum (tensile) and minimum (compressive) stress values were also calculated after varying print layer thickness as shown in Fig. 8(c) and 8(d). As the stress was closely related to the lithium-ion concentration during discharge, most delithiated carbon fibers at the layer thickness of about 0.4 mm resulted in more uniform stress distribution, yielding lowest tensile and compressive stress values. This indicated that an optimal layer thickness for carbon fiber structural battery composites not only resulted in highest electrochemical performance but also showed lowest concentrated stress values, thus lowering the possibility of mechanical damage due to high delithiation-induced mechanical stress and deformation.

A higher fiber volume fraction was typically preferred for carbon fiber composites due to increased mechanical performance. However, as found above, high fiber volume fraction may limit the lithium-ion transport and thus lower the obtained electrochemical performance attributed to low SPE ionic conductivity. Using the validated multiphysics model, the effect of SPE ionic conductivity was further investigated. The optimal layer thickness of 0.4 mm was selected and compared with the lowest layer thickness of 0.3 mm, which yielded the highest fiber volume fraction of 54 vol% in this study. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), after increasing the ionic conductivity from 2.0 mS/cm to 1.0 S/cm, a uniform lithium-ion concentration distribution was achieved across all carbon fiber layers regardless of print layer thickness, as further characterized in Fig. 9(b). At high ionic conductivity, the lithium ion can easily reach all carbon fibers, regardless of transport distance and gap left between carbon fibers. Due to the nature of carbon fiber negative electrode, the delithiation still started from outer surface, thus showing higher lithium-ion concentration in the center of each carbon fiber. The predicted cell voltage during discharge was also higher and became more stable with no obvious difference observed between samples printed with different layer thickness. A more uniformly distributed stress was also found within the structural battery composite samples during discharge in Fig. 9(d). As carbon fiber negative electrodes were more thoroughly delithiated, much lower lithium concentration was achieved, thus greatly reducing the corresponding stress values for σ11 in Fig. 9(e) and σ22 in Fig. 9(f). No obvious difference in predicted stress values was found between samples printed with different layer thickness, attributed to greatly increased ionic conductivity and more uniform lithium concentration at the SOC of 1.00. This suggested that improving SPE ionic conductivity remained a critical factor affecting the multifunctional performance of carbon fiber structural battery composites. It should be noted that a relatively high theoretical ionic conductivity was projected with the proposed multiphysics model and highlighted the importance of improving ionic conductivity of electrolyte for future investigations of the multifunctional performance of structural battery composites.

Despite relatively low SPE ionic conductivity, lowering discharge rate may also contribute to more uniform lithium concentration and thus was further investigated. After decreasing discharge rate from 1C to 0.2C, the distribution of lithium concentrations became more uniform both for samples with layer thickness of 0.4 mm and 0.3 mm in Fig. 10 (a). The lithium concentration still tended to be lower toward the separator with a higher gradient of lithium concentration observed for layer thickness of 0.3 mm in Fig. 10(b). At a lower discharge rate, higher discharge capacity was also achieved but the sample with smaller print layer thickness (i.e., 0.3 mm) showed slightly lower electrochemical performance in Fig. 10(c). The resulting stress distribution was also compared in Fig. 10(d). A relatively uniform stress distribution was observed for the sample with print thickness of 0.4 mm due to uniform lithium concentration. However, for the sample with print thickness of 0.3 mm, the stress distribution still tended to be higher toward the end further away from the separator. As summarized in Fig. 10(e) and 10(f), decreasing discharge rate significantly reduced the resulting maximum stress values. However, the sample with smaller layer thickness obviously yielded higher stress values due to more nonuniform lithium concentrations. Although decreasing discharge rate improved delithiation of carbon fiber layers within the negative electrode, the relatively lower SPE ionic conductivity still dominated the discharge process, showing the importance of achieving uniform charge transfer to improve multifunctional performance of carbon fiber structural battery composites. Previous studies [33] typically used low discharge rate to obtain improved electrochemical performance. However, such improvement will be lowered if smaller layer thickness is implemented due to increasingly nonuniform discharge process.

In this study, a new drop-on-demand AM method and a multiphysics numerical model were combined to investigate multifunctional performance of carbon fiber structural battery composite. The drop-on-demand AM approach was first developed to fabricate carbon fiber structural battery composites. The desized carbon fibers were directly used as negative electrode. Binder-free cathode materials were coated on carbon fibers using a EPD method before being used as positive electrode. The drop-on-demand method allowed selective deposition of SPE and photopolymer solutions at different layers to fabricate a complete multifunctional structural battery composite. An integrated in-situ consolidation technique enabled an accurate control of layer thickness during 3D printing. Decreasing layer thickness from 0.7 mm to 0.3 mm increased fiber volume fraction from 18 vol% to 54 vol%. The resulting flexural modulus and strength were increased by 16-fold and 6-fold, respectively. The corresponding discharge capacity and energy density were found to be initially improved by 47% and 39%, respectively. However, further increasing fiber volume fraction lowered the obtained electrochemical properties due to limited lithium transport within closely packed carbon fibers. A multiphysics numerical model was also implemented to study the effect of print layer thickness on the multifunctional performance of the 3D printed carbon fiber composites. A good agreement was found between the prediction results and the experimental measurements. An optimal print layer thickness of 0.4 mm was found with the highest discharge capacity and energy density obtained at a fiber volume fraction of 35 vol%. The discharge process was dominated by a nonuniform distribution of lithium-ion concentration due to low SPE ionic conductivity. The resulting stress distribution was also found to be concentrated within carbon fiber layers with highest gradient of lithium-ion concentration. Increasing ionic conductivity significantly improved the discharge process with no obvious difference observed in lithium-ion concentration for samples printed with different layer thickness and fiber volume fraction. Decreasing discharge rate also improved both electrochemical and mechanical performance. However, the structural battery composite samples with smaller layer thickness still showed relatively lower multifunctional performance. These findings provided insights in future design and fabrication of carbon fiber structural battery composites with high electrochemical and mechanical performance for multifunctional applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from AFOSR grant FA9550-21-1-0226.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2023.111888.
